name: The Register Collaboration
description: Theodric has been receiving names from a passage entrance at the bone fields' edge for twelve years. Elder Thrum has been leaving them. Neither has ever spoken to the other. Take Thrum's address to Theodric.
accept_text: She has already found a piece of paper before she finished speaking — the logistics of communication are clearly something she thinks about practically. 'My name and the passage entrance coordinates, in the notation that is legible from the surface side.' She writes it with the economical script of someone who has written many things in small spaces. 'We have records he does not have. He has records we do not have. Twelve years of name-exchange through a passage entrance.' She folds the paper. 'The exchange has been useful but it has been slow. He leaves a list. We read it. We find what we can from our records. We leave names at the entrance. He comes to the entrance.' She pauses. 'I would like to meet him. I would like to show him the section of the transit way that runs beneath the field he tends. I think he would find it useful, and I think — I believe — that a person who has spent thirty-one years doing what he does would understand the value of what is below.' She holds out the paper. 'Tell him: the passage is safe and passable, the route takes forty minutes, and if he is willing to descend I will have tea prepared. I have been informed that this is a surface custom that indicates serious welcome.'
complete_text: Theodric reads the paper with the same thorough attention he gives to archival documentation, which means he reads it twice. Then he looks up with an expression that is doing several things at once — none of which are displeasure. 'Elder Thrum.' He says the name as if confirming it after having referred to the source of the names only as the deep for twelve years. 'Forty minutes through the passage.' He looks at the field. 'I have been coming to this field twice a year for thirty-one years and someone has been forty minutes beneath me for twelve of those years, reading my lists and contributing names.' He is quiet. 'I know the passage entrance. I've examined it from the outside. I've been — careful. About going in. The bone fields are my work and whatever is below is different work, and I have not known who does that work.' He folds the paper carefully. 'She has the transit-way records.' He says it mostly to himself. 'Forty-three names in twelve years from the transit records alone. If the transit documentation covers the full pre-battle period —' He stops. 'That's the gap I've never been able to close. The identity records after the battle broke down. The records before the battle — the people as they were before they came here — I haven't been able to find those.' He looks at the paper. 'Tell her: yes. Tell her I'll come on the next visit.' He pauses. 'And tell her — the tea is not necessary but I would not refuse it. Thirty-one years is a long time to tend the same field. Meeting someone who has been helping from below for twelve of those years is — I think I'd like to sit down for that.'
